concepts.title: Intersection of line
concepts.conceptBody:
The ratio in which a given line divides the line segment joining two points 

Take the given line as $\text{L}:\text{ax}+\text{by}+\text{c}=0$ and the two points be $\text{P}\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}}{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)\And \text{Q}\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}}{{\text{y}}_{2}} \right)$ Then the line intersects the segment $\text{PQ}$ in the ratio $\text{m}:\text{n}$, which is given by

$\frac{\text{m}}{\text{n}}=-\left( \frac{\text{a}{{\text{x}}_{1}}+\text{bx}{{\text{y}}_{1}}+\text{c}}{\text{a}{{\text{x}}_{2}}+\text{b}{{\text{y}}_{2}}+\text{c}} \right)$

Now see the significance of negative sign

As shown in figure $1\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ L}$ divides $\text{PQ}$ internally in the ratio $\frac{\text{m}}{\text{n}}$ which should be positive.

Also, P & Q lie on opposite sides of line L.

Hence their signs will also be opposite

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ for internal division $\frac{\text{m}}{\text{n}}>0,\text{a}{{\text{x}}_{1}}+\text{b}{{\text{y}}_{1}}+\text{c}\And \text{a}{{\text{x}}_{2}}+\text{b}{{\text{y}}_{2}}+\text{c}$ have opposite sign.

external division $\frac{m}{n}<0a{{x}_{1}}+{{b}_{1}}+c\And {{a}_{2}}+{{b}_{2}}+c$ have same sign.

You can see that in case of external division $\text{P}\And \text{Q}$ lie on the same side of line $\text{L}$.

The length of perpendicular from a point on a line 

(i) The length of the perpendicular from the point $\left( \alpha ,\beta \right)$ to the line $ax+by+c=0$ is given by

$\text{p}=\frac{\left| \text{a}\alpha +\text{b}\beta +\text{c} \right|}{\sqrt{{{\text{a}}^{2}}+{{\text{b}}^{2}}}}$

(ii) The length of the perpendicular from the origin $\left( 0,0 \right)$ to the line $ax+by+c=0$ is given by

$\text{p}=\frac{\left| \text{c} \right|}{\sqrt{{{\text{a}}^{2}}+{{\text{b}}^{2}}}}$

(iii) The distance between two parallel lines $ax+by+{{c}_{1}}=0$ and $ax+by+{{c}_{2}}=0$ is given by

$\text{d}=\frac{\left| {{\text{c}}_{1}}-{{\text{c}}_{2}} \right|}{\sqrt{{{\text{a}}^{2}}+{{\text{b}}^{2}}}}$

(iv) The distance between two parallel lines can also be obtained by taking a suitable point (take $\text{y}=0$ and find $\text{x}$ or take $\text{x}=0$ and find $\text{y}$ ) on one straight line and then finding the length of the perpendicular from this point to the second line.

(v) Area of a parallelogram or a rhombus equation of whose sides are given, can be obtained by using the following formula

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Area }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }=\frac{{{p}_{1}}{{p}_{2}}}{\text{sin}\theta }$

concepts.title: position of points wrt line
concepts.conceptBody:
Position of point relative to a given line 

Let a given line be $\text{L}:\text{ax}+\text{by}+\text{c}=0$

Also assume two points $\text{P}\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}}{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$$\text{Q}\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}}{{\text{y}}_{2}} \right)$

  • Then $P\And Q$ lie on same side of line $\text{L}$ if $\left( \text{a}{{\text{x}}_{1}}+\text{b}{{\text{y}}_{1}}+\text{c} \right)\And \left( \text{a}{{\text{x}}_{2}}+\text{b}{{\text{y}}_{2}}+\text{c} \right)$ are of same sign

Or                    

  • $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Points $P\And Q$ lie on opposite side of line $L$ if $\left( \text{a}{{\text{x}}_{1}}+\text{b}{{\text{y}}_{2}}+\text{c} \right)\And \left( \text{a}{{\text{x}}_{2}}+\text{b}{{\text{y}}_{2}}+\text{c} \right)$ are of opposite signs. 
     

Or  
 

  • $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ For origin we just need to check the sign of constant c.

(a) A point $\text{P}\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}}{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$ will lie on the origin side of line $\text{L}$ if $a{{x}_{1}}+\text{b}{{\text{y}}_{1}}+\text{c}\And \text{c}$ have same sign.

(b) A point $\text{P}$ will lie on non-origin side of line $\text{l}$ if $\text{a}a{{\text{x}}_{1}}+\text{b}{{\text{y}}_{1}}+\text{c}\And \text{c}$ have opposite sign.

concepts.title: Image of a point
concepts.conceptBody:
Some Special Type of Questions 

I. Foot of perpendicular

In this type you have to find the foot of perpendicular drawn from the point $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}}{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$ to the line $\text{ax}+\text{by}+\text{c}=0$

Working Rule 

  1. Let $\text{R}$ be $\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}},{{\text{y}}_{2}} \right)$ which we have to fond.
  2. Put $R$ in $ax+by+c=0$ to get an equation
  3. Since $\text{PR}\bot $ given line, apply ${{\text{m}}_{1}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}_{2}}=-1$, to get slope of $\text{PR}$
  4. Get second equation in terms of ${{\text{x}}_{2}}{{\text{y}}_{2}}$ by using slope relation. From step $2\And 4$ get the values of $\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}}{{\text{y}}_{2}} \right)$

II. Similar to the previous type is image or reflection of a point $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}}{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$ about a line mirror.

Our aim is to find Q.

Working Rule 

  1. Let $\text{Q}$ be $\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}}{{\text{y}}_{2}} \right)$
  2. Since $\text{Q}$ is the image of $\text{P},\text{Q}$ is equidistant from line as $\text{P}$ is from the given line.

Hence $\text{R}$ is the midpoint of $\text{PQ}\And $ it lies on the line ax + by $+\text{c}=0$

  1. Put $\text{R}$ in the line to get an equation in $\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}}{{\text{y}}_{2}} \right)$
  2. Also $\text{PQ}$ is perpendicular to given line $\text{ax}+\text{by}+\text{c}=0$. So, equate slopes $\left( {{\text{m}}_{1}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}_{2}}=-1 \right)$ to get another relation in $\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}}{{\text{y}}_{2}} \right)$.

Solve step 3 & 4 to get $\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}}{{\text{y}}_{2}} \right)$.
